questions.title: Question 1
questions.question: Which SQL warehouse type provides near-instant startup with no cold start delay?

choices.B: Pro
choices.C: Serverless
choices.D: All warehouse types have instant startup

questions.shortAnswer:
Serverless SQL warehouses provide near-instant startup because Databricks manages the compute
infrastructure in advance. Classic and Pro warehouses require 2–5 minutes to provision cluster
nodes before queries can run.

questions.title: Question 2
questions.question: A team needs 8 concurrent analyst sessions without queue buildup. Which warehouse configuration supports this best?

choices.A: Classic — single cluster with extra memory
choices.B: Pro — multi-cluster auto-scaling up to 10 clusters
choices.C: X-Large Classic — larger size reduces queuing
choices.D: Assign personal clusters to each analyst

questions.shortAnswer:
Pro SQL warehouses support multi-cluster scaling. When one cluster reaches capacity, additional
clusters spin up automatically (up to the configured maximum of 10). Classic warehouses are
single-cluster only — every session queues behind others when that cluster is at capacity.

questions.title: Question 3
questions.question: What does the SQL warehouse auto-stop feature do?

choices.A: Stops all running queries after the configured timeout
choices.B: Shuts down warehouse compute when idle for the configured duration (default 120 minutes)
choices.C: Stops only Serverless warehouse queries
choices.D: Prevents a warehouse from starting if another is already running

questions.shortAnswer:
Auto-stop conserves cost by terminating warehouse compute when no queries have run for the
idle timeout period. The default is 120 minutes. Running queries are never stopped by auto-stop
— it only triggers when the warehouse is idle.

questions.title: Question 4
questions.question: An analyst needs to connect Power BI Desktop to a Databricks SQL warehouse. Which connection protocol should they use?

choices.B: ODBC or JDBC driver provided by Databricks
choices.C: REST API with JSON output
choices.D: Direct cloud storage access (S3/ADLS)

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks provides ODBC and JDBC drivers compatible with standard BI tools including Power BI,
Tableau, and Excel. Delta Sharing is for cross-organization data sharing, not BI tool connectivity.

questions.title: Question 5
questions.question: Row-level security via Unity Catalog row access policies is available on which SQL warehouse types?

choices.B: Pro and Serverless
choices.C: Classic only
choices.D: All warehouse types equally

questions.shortAnswer:
Row-level security (implemented through Unity Catalog row access policies) requires Pro or
Serverless SQL warehouses. Classic warehouses do not support Unity Catalog's fine-grained
access controls such as row filters and column masks.

questions.title: Question 6
questions.question: Which Databricks SQL feature helps identify the most expensive queries by cost and duration?

choices.B: Query History — shows runtime, bytes scanned, and user
choices.C: Cluster event logs
choices.D: MLflow experiment tracking

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL Query History tracks all queries with execution time, bytes scanned, queue time,
and the user who ran them. Admins can filter by warehouse, user, and time range to identify
expensive or slow queries.

questions.title: Question 7
questions.question: Partner Connect in Databricks is used to:

choices.A: Connect multiple Databricks workspaces to each other
choices.B: Establish pre-built integrations with partner tools like dbt, Fivetran, and Tableau with automated credential setup
choices.C: Share data with external organizations via Delta Sharing
choices.D: Connect to on-premises databases over a VPN

questions.shortAnswer:
Partner Connect provides one-click integration setup for certified Databricks partner tools. It
automatically creates service principals, generates access tokens, and configures the partner
tool's connection — eliminating manual credential management.

questions.title: Question 8
questions.question: A Serverless SQL warehouse has been idle for 10 minutes with no active queries. What happens next?

choices.A: It continues running indefinitely until manually stopped
choices.B: It automatically scales down and stops, then restarts near-instantly on the next query
choices.C: It generates a timeout error for the next submitted query
choices.D: It must be restarted manually from the SQL Warehouses UI

questions.shortAnswer:
Serverless warehouses auto-stop when idle (default 10 minutes for Serverless vs 120 for
Classic/Pro). Because compute is managed by Databricks, Serverless warehouses restart
near-instantly on the next query — there is no cold-start wait as with Classic/Pro.

questions.title: Question 9
questions.question: Which SQL warehouse size is most appropriate for a single analyst running exploratory queries on 10 GB of data?

choices.B: X-Small or Small — cost-efficient for single-user exploratory work
choices.C: X-Large — minimum recommended size for any analytical query
choices.D: Size does not affect query performance for Delta tables

questions.shortAnswer:
X-Small or Small warehouses are cost-efficient for individual analysts running exploratory queries
on moderate data sizes. Larger warehouse sizes are justified for complex queries on very large
datasets (100+ GB) or for supporting many concurrent users.

questions.title: Question 10
questions.question: A SQL query in Databricks SQL reads from a Delta table. The identical query runs again 30 seconds later with no data changes in between. What happens?

choices.A: The query re-executes fully on the warehouse
choices.B: Results are returned from the query result cache, bypassing warehouse compute
choices.C: The second query fails due to a caching conflict
choices.D: Query caching only activates after a minimum of 5 minutes between runs

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL maintains a query result cache. When the same query runs within the cache TTL
and the underlying Delta table version has not changed, results are returned from cache
instantly — no compute is used. The cache is automatically invalidated when the table is written to.

questions.title: Question 11
questions.question: What is the Unity Catalog three-level namespace?

choices.B: catalog.schema.table
choices.C: metastore.catalog.table
choices.D: environment.schema.table

questions.shortAnswer:
Unity Catalog uses `catalog.schema.table`. Schemas in Unity Catalog are equivalent to databases
in the legacy two-level Hive metastore. The metastore is the top-level container that holds
catalogs, but it is not part of the object-reference namespace.

questions.title: Question 12
questions.question: A managed Delta table is dropped with `DROP TABLE`. What happens to the underlying data files?

choices.A: Files remain in DBFS and must be deleted manually
choices.B: Files are moved to a recycle bin and retained for 30 days
choices.C: Files are permanently deleted along with the table metadata
choices.D: Files are archived in cloud storage for 90 days

questions.shortAnswer:
Dropping a managed table removes both the table definition from the metastore AND the
underlying data files from cloud storage. This operation is irreversible without a backup.
External tables behave differently — only their metadata is removed, leaving files intact.

questions.title: Question 13
questions.question: Which GRANT statement gives a user the ability to read data from a Delta table in Unity Catalog?

choices.A: `GRANT READ ON TABLE catalog.schema.table TO user`
choices.B: `GRANT VIEW ON TABLE catalog.schema.table TO user`
choices.C: `GRANT SELECT ON TABLE catalog.schema.table TO user`
choices.D: `GRANT ACCESS ON TABLE catalog.schema.table TO user`

questions.shortAnswer:
`SELECT` is the correct privilege name for read access in Unity Catalog. `READ`, `VIEW`, and
`ACCESS` are not valid Unity Catalog privilege names. Users also typically need `USE CATALOG`
and `USE SCHEMA` on the parent objects to resolve the table.

questions.title: Question 14
questions.question: A data engineer appends data with new columns to an existing Delta table. Which write option enables schema evolution?

choices.B: `.option("mergeSchema", "true")`
choices.C: `.option("allowNewColumns", "true")`
choices.D: No option is needed — Delta always accepts new columns

questions.shortAnswer:
`mergeSchema=true` (or `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` globally)
enables schema evolution during writes. New columns in the incoming DataFrame are added to the
Delta table schema automatically. Without this option, a write containing extra columns raises a
schema mismatch error.

questions.title: Question 15
questions.question: An external table pointing to `gs://bucket/data/` is dropped with `DROP TABLE`. What happens to the files in GCS?

choices.A: Files are deleted from GCS immediately
choices.B: Files remain in GCS — only the table metadata entry is removed
choices.C: Files are moved to a Databricks-managed DBFS location
choices.D: Files are archived by Databricks for 30 days

questions.shortAnswer:
External tables do not own their data. `DROP TABLE` on an external table removes only the
catalog metadata entry (table definition). The actual data files in the external storage location
are completely unaffected.

questions.title: Question 16
questions.question: In Unity Catalog, what does `USE CATALOG my_catalog` accomplish?

choices.B: Sets `my_catalog` as the default catalog for the current session
choices.C: Grants the current user access to `my_catalog`
choices.D: Lists all objects in `my_catalog`

questions.shortAnswer:
`USE CATALOG` sets the session's default catalog. Subsequent queries that use two-level names
(`schema.table`) or single-level names (`table`) resolve against this default catalog. It does
not create, grant access to, or list objects in the catalog.

questions.title: Question 17
questions.question: Which statement correctly describes Delta Lake ACID transactions?

choices.A: Delta Lake transactions are eventually consistent, not fully ACID
choices.B: Delta tables support Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability for all read and write operations
choices.C: ACID guarantees apply only to batch writes, not streaming writes
choices.D: ACID compliance requires a Unity Catalog metastore to be attached

questions.shortAnswer:
Delta Lake's transaction log (`_delta_log`) provides full ACID guarantees for all operations —
batch, streaming, and concurrent reads/writes. This is a core differentiator from plain
Parquet/CSV data lakes, which have no transactional guarantees.

questions.title: Question 18
questions.question: What does `SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE catalog.schema.table` return?

choices.A: The table's schema — column names and data types
choices.B: All privileges granted on the table and the principals they are granted to
choices.C: The table's storage location and file format
choices.D: Recent data access history for the table

questions.shortAnswer:
`SHOW GRANTS ON <object>` lists all Unity Catalog privileges granted on that specific object:
the privilege type (e.g., SELECT, MODIFY), the principal (user, group, or service principal),
and the grantor. It is the primary tool for auditing object-level permissions.

questions.title: Question 19
questions.question:
A data engineer runs:

```sql
ALTER TABLE t SET TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration' = 'interval 30 days'
);
```

What does this configure?

choices.A: VACUUM will retain deleted data files for at least 30 days before removing them
choices.B: The table itself expires and is deleted after 30 days
choices.C: Deleted data rows are retained for 30 days and can be restored
choices.D: The Delta transaction log is compacted and retained for 30 days

questions.shortAnswer:
`delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration` sets the minimum file retention period for VACUUM.
VACUUM will not delete files newer than this threshold, enabling time travel queries up to
30 days into the past. The default is 7 days.

questions.title: Question 20
questions.question: Which window function returns the value from the next row in the result set?

choices.B: `LEAD(col, 1)`
choices.C: `NEXT(col)`
choices.D: `OFFSET(col, -1)`

questions.shortAnswer:
`LEAD(column, offset)` returns the value from `offset` rows ahead of the current row within
the partition. `LAG` looks backward (previous rows). `NEXT` and `OFFSET` are not valid
Databricks SQL window functions.

questions.title: Question 21
questions.question: When two rows have the same value, which function produces gaps in the sequence of rank numbers?

choices.B: `RANK()` — tied rows receive the same rank; the next rank skips
choices.C: `DENSE_RANK()` — always produces gaps between all ranks
choices.D: Both `RANK()` and `ROW_NUMBER()` produce gaps for ties

questions.shortAnswer:
`RANK()` gives tied rows the same rank number and then skips the next rank value (e.g., 1, 1,
3). `ROW_NUMBER()` assigns unique sequential numbers regardless of ties — no gaps, no
duplicates. `DENSE_RANK()` gives tied rows the same rank but never skips (e.g., 1, 1, 2).

questions.title: Question 22
questions.question: A query uses `SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region)` with no `ORDER BY` clause. What does each row receive?

choices.A: A running total that accumulates within the region
choices.B: The total sum for the entire region — the same value for every row in that region
choices.C: NULL — `ORDER BY` is required for all window functions
choices.D: Each row's individual `revenue` value unchanged

questions.shortAnswer:
Without `ORDER BY` in the window specification, `SUM() OVER (PARTITION BY ...)` computes the
aggregate over the entire partition and returns that single value for every row in the partition.
Adding `ORDER BY` changes the frame to a running (cumulative) sum from the first row to the
current row.

questions.title: Question 23
questions.question: Which JOIN type returns rows from the left table only when no match exists in the right table?

choices.B: LEFT JOIN with `WHERE right_key IS NULL` — this is the only way
choices.C: LEFT ANTI JOIN
choices.D: EXCEPT

questions.shortAnswer:
`LEFT ANTI JOIN` explicitly returns left-table rows where no matching right-table row exists.
It is semantically equivalent to `LEFT JOIN ... WHERE right_key IS NULL` but more readable and
sometimes more efficient. `EXCEPT` operates on full-row set differences, not join conditions.

questions.title: Question 24
questions.question: An analyst writes `WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY date DESC) = 1`. The query fails. What is the correct approach?

choices.B: Wrap in a CTE and filter on the window result in the outer query, or use `QUALIFY`
choices.C: Use the `FILTER` keyword directly after the window function
choices.D: Add `GROUP BY` before the `WHERE` clause

questions.shortAnswer:
Window functions are evaluated after `WHERE` is applied, so you cannot filter on window
function results in `WHERE`. Two correct approaches:
questions.explanation:
- Wrap in a CTE: `WITH cte AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) AS rn FROM t) SELECT * FROM cte WHERE rn = 1`
- Use `QUALIFY`: `SELECT * FROM t QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) = 1`

questions.title: Question 25
questions.question: Which function flattens a nested array `[[1, 2], [3, 4]]` into `[1, 2, 3, 4]`?

choices.B: `FLATTEN(array_col)`
choices.C: `COLLECT_LIST(array_col)`
choices.D: `ARRAY_UNION(array_col)`

questions.shortAnswer:
`FLATTEN` merges a nested array-of-arrays into a single-level array. `EXPLODE` creates one
row per element of the outer array — it does not merge inner arrays. `COLLECT_LIST` and
`ARRAY_UNION` are aggregation functions, not array transformation functions.

questions.title: Question 26
questions.question: What is the difference between `COLLECT_LIST(product)` and `COLLECT_SET(product)`?

choices.B: `COLLECT_LIST` includes duplicate values; `COLLECT_SET` returns only unique values
choices.C: They are identical — both return all values in the group
choices.D: `COLLECT_SET` is faster; `COLLECT_LIST` is used for large datasets only

questions.shortAnswer:
`COLLECT_LIST` aggregates all values including duplicates into an array.
`COLLECT_SET` returns only distinct values. Neither guarantees a specific ordering of
elements in the resulting array.

questions.title: Question 27
questions.question: A CTE is defined as `WITH sales AS (SELECT ...)`. How many times can `sales` be referenced in the main query body?

choices.B: Multiple times — CTEs can be referenced as many times as needed in the same query
choices.C: Only in the `FROM` clause, once
choices.D: CTEs cannot be reused — use subqueries for any reuse pattern

questions.shortAnswer:
CTEs can be referenced multiple times within the same query body. This is a key advantage
over inline subqueries for readability and avoiding repetition. A CTE defined in a `WITH`
block is scoped to the single query statement.

questions.title: Question 28
questions.question: Which of the following aggregate functions ignores NULL values?

choices.B: `SUM(column)`, `AVG(column)`, `MIN(column)`, `MAX(column)`, and `COUNT(column)`
choices.C: `COUNT(*)` ignores NULLs; all other aggregates treat NULLs as zero
choices.D: None — all aggregates in Databricks SQL include NULLs in their calculations

questions.shortAnswer:
`SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, and `COUNT(column)` all skip NULL values — NULLs are excluded
from the calculation entirely. Only `COUNT(*)` counts NULLs because it counts rows, not
column values. NULLs are never treated as 0 in standard SQL aggregation.

questions.title: Question 29
questions.question: `DATEDIFF(end_date, start_date)` — what does this function return in Databricks SQL?

choices.A: The number of seconds between the two dates
choices.B: The number of calendar days from `start_date` to `end_date`
choices.C: The number of full months between the two dates
choices.D: A formatted string representing the interval

questions.shortAnswer:
`DATEDIFF(end, start)` in Databricks SQL returns the number of calendar days between the
two dates as an integer. Note: the end date is the first argument and start date is the
second — the reverse of some other SQL dialects.

questions.title: Question 30
questions.question: Which SQL expression returns the current timestamp in Databricks SQL?

choices.B: `SYSDATE()`
choices.C: `current_timestamp()`
choices.D: `NOW()` — only this form is valid

questions.shortAnswer:
`current_timestamp()` is the canonical Databricks SQL function for the current timestamp.
`NOW()` is also accepted as a synonym. `GETDATE()` is SQL Server syntax and `SYSDATE()` is
Oracle syntax — neither is native to Databricks SQL.

questions.title: Question 31
questions.question: Table A has 100 rows and table B has 50 rows. A `CROSS JOIN` is performed. How many rows are in the result?

choices.B: 150 rows
choices.C: 5,000 rows
choices.D: 50 rows

questions.shortAnswer:
A `CROSS JOIN` produces the Cartesian product of both tables: every row in A is combined
with every row in B. 100 × 50 = 5,000 rows. Cross joins are intentional when you need
all combinations (e.g., generating date-product combinations).

questions.title: Question 32
questions.question: An analyst needs to filter rows where a window function result equals 1. Which SQL clause is designed for this purpose?

choices.B: `WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) = 1`
choices.C: `QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) = 1`
choices.D: `FILTER ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) = 1`

questions.shortAnswer:
`QUALIFY` is the SQL clause specifically designed to filter on window function results. It
evaluates after window functions are computed, allowing direct filtering on their output.
`WHERE` and `HAVING` are evaluated before window functions — they cannot reference window
function results. `FILTER` modifies aggregate functions, not window functions.

questions.title: Question 33
questions.question: Which dashboard widget is designed to display a single KPI value prominently, such as "Total Revenue: $1.2M"?

choices.B: Text widget
choices.C: Counter widget
choices.D: Pivot widget

questions.shortAnswer:
Counter widgets are purpose-built for displaying a single prominent metric value. They are
typically used for KPIs like total revenue, active user count, or daily order volume. A Text
widget displays static markdown; a Table widget shows tabular data.

questions.title: Question 34
questions.question: When is a pie chart the most appropriate visualization choice?

choices.A: When showing a metric trend over time
choices.B: When showing parts of a whole that sum to 100%, with fewer than 6 slices
choices.C: When comparing absolute values across many categories
choices.D: When one or more values are negative

questions.shortAnswer:
Pie charts work best for part-to-whole relationships with few categories (typically fewer than
6). With more categories, slices become too small to distinguish. Pie charts are not appropriate
for time series, negative values, or comparisons where precise magnitude matters.

questions.title: Question 35
questions.question: How is a refresh schedule configured on a Databricks SQL dashboard?

choices.A: In the dashboard settings, set the refresh interval and assign a SQL warehouse
choices.B: Create a Databricks Job that calls the dashboard API at the desired interval
choices.C: Enable auto-refresh in the notebook that provides the dashboard's data
choices.D: Dashboards cannot have refresh schedules — only individual queries can be scheduled

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL dashboards have a built-in schedule configuration. From the dashboard settings,
set the interval (e.g., daily at 8 AM) and select which SQL warehouse will execute the
underlying queries. No external Job or notebook is required.

questions.title: Question 36
questions.question: What is the `{{parameter_name}}` syntax used for in Databricks SQL queries?

choices.A: Variable substitution in Python notebook cells
choices.B: Query parameters — replaced with user-selected values at query runtime
choices.C: Column aliasing in SELECT statements
choices.D: Referencing the output of a previously run query

questions.shortAnswer:
Double curly braces `{{parameter_name}}` are Databricks SQL's query parameter (mustache)
syntax. When a query containing this syntax is added to a dashboard, a corresponding filter
widget is automatically created. The selected value is injected into the SQL at runtime.

questions.title: Question 37
questions.question: A SQL alert has the condition `WHEN VALUE > 1000`. The query returns 850. Does the alert fire?

choices.A: Yes — 850 is close enough to 1000 to trigger
choices.B: No — 850 does not satisfy the condition `> 1000`
choices.C: Yes — the alert fires whenever the scheduled query runs, regardless of the returned value
choices.D: The alert returns an error because the threshold was not met

questions.shortAnswer:
The alert evaluates its threshold condition against the query result. Since 850 is not greater
than 1000, the condition is false and no notification is sent. The alert re-evaluates on its
next scheduled refresh.

questions.title: Question 38
questions.question: Which notification destination is NOT natively supported by Databricks SQL alerts?

choices.B: Slack via webhook
choices.C: PagerDuty
choices.D: SMS text message

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL alerts natively support email, Slack (via webhook), PagerDuty, and custom
webhooks. SMS is not a built-in destination. SMS notifications could be achieved by routing
through a custom webhook to an SMS gateway service.

questions.title: Question 39
questions.question: A Lakeview Dashboard is shared with a user with "Can View" permission. What can that user do?

choices.B: View the dashboard, interact with filter widgets, and export data — but not modify queries or layout
choices.C: Create new visualization widgets on the dashboard
choices.D: Change the dashboard's refresh schedule

questions.shortAnswer:
"Can View" permission allows a user to view the dashboard and interact with filter parameters.
They can also export data from individual visualizations. They cannot edit SQL queries, change
the layout, add widgets, or modify the refresh schedule.

questions.title: Question 40
questions.question: An analyst creates a line chart with `date` on the X-axis and `revenue` on the Y-axis. They want separate lines per region. Which configuration achieves this?

choices.A: Set "Group By" or "Series" to the `region` column in the chart configuration
choices.B: Create a separate chart for each region manually
choices.C: Write a `PIVOT` in the underlying SQL to create one column per region
choices.D: Set the chart color scheme to map colors by `revenue` value

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL chart visualizations have a "Group By" or "Series" configuration option. Setting
it to the `region` column automatically creates one line per distinct region value. This
approach is more maintainable than pivoting in SQL, as new regions appear automatically.

questions.title: Question 41
questions.question: A dashboard filter passes a selected date to all underlying queries. Which SQL snippet correctly uses this parameter?

choices.B: `WHERE order_date = {{selected_date}}`
choices.C: `WHERE order_date = @selected_date`
choices.D: `WHERE order_date = $selected_date`

questions.shortAnswer:
`{{parameter_name}}` is the Databricks SQL parameter substitution syntax. The value selected
in the dashboard filter widget is injected into the query at runtime. The colon (`:`) syntax
is used in some other SQL tools but not Databricks SQL.

questions.title: Question 42
questions.question: An analyst needs a summary table refreshed nightly without manually running the query. What is the most appropriate approach?

choices.A: Open Databricks SQL each morning and run the query manually
choices.B: Schedule the query in Databricks SQL to run nightly at midnight using a SQL warehouse
choices.C: Write a Python script that runs on the analyst's laptop at midnight
choices.D: Use Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines — it is the only supported option for automated query execution

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL has built-in query scheduling. Configure a schedule (e.g., nightly at midnight),
select a SQL warehouse, and the platform runs the query automatically. No external scripts,
jobs, or Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines are required for simple scheduled query execution.

questions.title: Question 43
questions.question: A sales dashboard shows different data per sales rep based on who is logged in. This behavior is implemented with:

choices.A: Separate dashboard copies for each sales rep
choices.B: A dashboard parameter pre-populated with the logged-in user's name
choices.C: A Unity Catalog row-level security policy on the underlying table
choices.D: A Python UDF in the dashboard query that filters by session user

questions.shortAnswer:
Unity Catalog row-level security policies automatically filter rows based on the current
user's identity at query time. The dashboard query returns data appropriate for the logged-in
user with no dashboard-level configuration needed. This is more secure than parameter-based
filtering, which a user could potentially bypass.

questions.title: Question 44
questions.question: A query result cache serves stale data after an ETL pipeline writes new data to the underlying Delta table. What invalidates the cache?

choices.B: Writing to the underlying Delta table — cache invalidation is automatic
choices.C: Running a manual cache clear command on the warehouse
choices.D: Restarting the SQL warehouse

questions.shortAnswer:
Databricks SQL result cache tracks Delta table versions via the transaction log. When new data
is written to a Delta table (a new transaction is logged), the cache for all queries touching
that table is automatically invalidated. The next query re-executes against fresh data.

questions.title: Question 45
questions.question: Which approach is the documented and safest fit?

choices.A: Standard view: `CREATE VIEW gold.daily_revenue AS SELECT date, SUM(amount) ... GROUP BY date`
choices.B: Materialized view: `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW gold.daily_revenue AS ...` with an hourly refresh schedule
choices.C: Pin the dashboard to a Pro SQL Warehouse with result caching enabled
choices.D: Convert the orders table to bucketing on date

questions.shortAnswer: Genie Spaces is the documented natural-language analytics surface for exactly this use case. UC permissions, including column masks and row filters, apply to every Genie-generated SQL query — so CS users physically cannot see PII columns they're not granted on, regardless of how Genie phrases the question. Option A reinvents Genie poorly. Option C duplicates data and creates a refresh / drift problem. Option D is a retrieval-augmentation pattern over *unstructured* data — vector search returns similar rows by embedding distance, which is the wrong tool for structured analytic queries (counts, aggregates, joins).
